ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for a multi-component fried chicken tenders combo meal. FOODON_00002490 (fried chicken food product) is the closest defensible anchor. The combo nature (fries, dip, drink) is not captured in the ontology.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP covers breaded fried chicken meat, which is the primary component. The combo meal side components (fries, ranch dip, soft drink) are not represented in a single FoodEx2 code. This is the closest defensible anchor for the dominant food component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for a fried chicken tenders combo meal. SNOMED CT clinical food coding does not extend to composite fast-food combo meals at this specificity.

serving.description: 1 set meal (4 tenders, regular fries, ranch dip, regular soft drink)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is a full combo set comprising four breaded and deep-fried chicken breast tenders tossed in wing sauce, a regular portion of french fries, a ranch dipping sauce, and a regular soft drink, totalling approximately 650 grams.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Chicken breast tenders, breaded, fried; French fries, fast food; Soft drink, cola; Ranch dressing — composite estimation
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific multi-component combo meal. Nutrient values per 100g were estimated by weighting USDA FoodData Central component entries: breaded fried chicken breast tenders (FDC ID 171477, approximately 200g of 650g total), fast-food french fries (FDC ID 170720, approximately 150g), ranch dressing (FDC ID 172237, approximately 30g), and a regular cola soft drink (FDC ID 174851, approximately 270g). The input record's per_100g values (energy 290 kcal, protein 22.5g, fat 17.5g, carbohydrate 12.5g, sodium 900mg) are broadly consistent with this weighted composite and have been retained with cross-reference to USDA component data. Singapore HPB Foodo database was also consulted for local fried chicken and fast-food analogues to validate plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — breaded fried chicken and french fries analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this specific combo meal. GI of 65 is estimated as a weighted composite: breaded fried chicken tenders approximate GI ~60 (analogous to battered/breaded chicken, University of Sydney GI Database), fast-food french fries GI ~63–75 (University of Sydney GI Database, mean ~70), and cola soft drink GI ~63 (University of Sydney GI Database). Weighted by approximate carbohydrate contribution per component across the 650g serving. The dominant carbohydrate contributors are fries and the soft drink. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (65 × 81.25) / 100 ≈ 52.8 for the full 650g serving. Note: the soft drink component (a sugar-sweetened beverage) significantly elevates the glycaemic load of the full combo.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium at 900 mg per 100g; a full 650g serving delivers approximately 5,850 mg sodium, nearly 2.5 times the WHO daily recommended limit of 2,000 mg
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this combo meal entirely; if consumed, request no added sauces, substitute water for the soft drink, and limit portion to a single tender without fries to reduce sodium intake to a manageable level.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened soft drink (SSB) component is a hard disqualifier; combined with high-GI french fries (GI ~70) and breaded chicken, the full combo delivers a very high glycaemic load of approximately 52.8 GL units per serving with low dietary fibre (1.0 g per 100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should not consume this combo as served; the sugar-sweetened drink must be replaced with water or unsweetened tea, the fries should be omitted or replaced with a salad, and wing sauces containing added sugar (teriyaki, sweet buffalo) should be avoided to prevent acute postprandial hyperglycaemia.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Trans fat at 0.25 g per 100g (1.625 g per full serving) from deep-frying, combined with saturated fat at 3.5 g per 100g, high sodium at 900 mg per 100g, and ultra-processed composition create a strongly atherogenic and hypertensive nutritional profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this combo meal; the combination of trans fat, saturated fat, high sodium, and a sugar-sweetened beverage collectively elevates LDL cholesterol, promotes arterial inflammation, and raises blood pressure, all of which are independent cardiovascular risk factors.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that worsen joint inflammation; trans fat at 0.25 g per 100g is a pro-inflammatory disqualifier for RA management
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried and trans-fat-containing foods such as this combo; replace with grilled chicken and a side salad to reduce the pro-inflammatory dietary burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: The 4 Piece Chicken Tenders Combo Meal is a Western-style fast-food combo meal served in Singapore, consisting of four pieces of breaded and deep-fried chicken breast tenders tossed in a choice of two wing flavours (commonly buffalo or teriyaki sauce), accompanied by a regular portion of french fries, a ranch dipping sauce, and a regular sugar-sweetened soft drink. The chicken tenders have a crispy, golden-brown breaded exterior with a juicy, tender chicken breast interior. The overall flavour profile is savoury, mildly spicy or sweet depending on the wing sauce chosen, with the ranch dip adding a cool, creamy, herbaceous contrast. The combo is classified as ultra-processed and is prepared entirely by deep-frying, which generates trans fats and advanced glycation end-products (AGEs). Per 100g, the meal delivers 290 kcal, 22.5g protein, 17.5g fat (including 3.5g saturated fat and 0.25g trans fat), 12.5g carbohydrate, 2.5g sugar, 1.0g dietary fibre, and 900mg sodium. The sodium content is extremely high, making this meal unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or fluid retention. The sugar-sweetened soft drink component is a hard disqualifier for Type 2 Diabetes suitability. The trans fat content and deep-fried preparation make this meal unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular disease. The glycaemic index of the combo is estimated at approximately 65 (medium), but the full-serving glycaemic load is very high at approximately 52.8 due to the large carbohydrate contribution from fries and the soft drink. This meal is halal-certified in the Singapore context and is commonly ordered as a lunch or dinner option from fast-food delivery platforms. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or individuals on low-sodium, low-fat, or diabetic dietary plans without significant modification.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values per 100g are estimated from a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central component entries (breaded fried chicken tenders, fast-food french fries, ranch dressing, cola soft drink) cross-referenced against the input record values. The input record values are broadly consistent with USDA component data and have been retained. No single approved-source entry exists for this exact multi-component combo meal. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component analogues. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on USDA FoodData Central component data weighted by approximate gram contribution of each component within the 650g full serving. The input record per_100g values were found to be broadly plausible and have been retained with USDA cross-reference validation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this specific composite fast-food combo meal are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo, or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16. Individual component data exists but cannot be reliably aggregated to a single per_100g composite figure without introducing unacceptable estimation error. 3. vitamin_c_mg is reported as 0.0 (confirmed trace/negligible): deep-frying destroys ascorbic acid; USDA FoodData Central reports 0.0 mg vitamin C for breaded fried chicken and fast-food fries. 4. Glycaemic load per full 650g serving is approximately 52.8 GL units, which is very high. The reported glycemic_load_per_serving field reflects the full 650g default serving. 5. The soft drink component is assumed to be a standard sugar-sweetened cola; if a diet or unsweetened drink is substituted, the sugar, carbohydrate, and GI profile would change substantially. 6. Trans fat is reported as 0.25g per 100g from the input record; this is consistent with residual trans fat from commercial deep-frying oils and is retained. The input record's rheumatoid arthritis caution cited 0.3g/100g, which is a minor discrepancy; the authoritative value used here is 0.25g/100g from the input per_100g field. 7. Two portions are noted: the full set meal (650g, default) and the chicken tenders only (200g). The per_100g values and GL calculation are based on the full 650g combo as the primary serving context. 8. This record is classified as a Singaporean fast-food dish (Western cuisine, served in Singapore); multilingual naming in English, Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi has been provided per G6 mandatory requirements.
